intro: The clearest split is that np and Capacitor overlap on scope but take different approaches. np is CI/CD for Applications tool for developers. Specializes in Release Tooling; Capacitor is Release / App Store Operations tool for developers. Specializes in Cross Platform Framework. The data points np at automated build/test/deploy pipelines and team collaboration on code, and Capacitor at mobile app release automation and over-the-air updates. Pricing: np is free, free tier; Capacitor is free, free tier. Lock-in is low for np and low for Capacitor, with DX at 5/5 vs 5/5 and transparency at 5/5 vs 5/5. On listed strengths, np emphasises automates app store submissions and release management, and Capacitor emphasises mobile app release automation and over-the-air updates. Neither lists an enterprise tier, so contract-level buying may require a direct conversation. Honest trade-off: np is not for web-only products without native app distribution; Capacitor is not for internal projects, so confirm the fit first, then weigh the pivotal factor above against your budget and lock-in tolerance.
quick_take: np is for mobile teams shipping frequent app updates; Capacitor is for mobile; decide based on which scope-fit aligns with your team.
verdict_a: Choose np if your workload matches mobile teams shipping frequent app updates and your work involves automated build/test/deploy pipelines. Skip it if your context is web-only products without native app distribution.
verdict_b: Choose Capacitor if your workload matches mobile and your work involves mobile app release automation. Skip it if your context is internal projects.

faq.a: Entry pricing is listed as unknown for np and unknown for Capacitor. Both offer a free tier for getting started. Total cost at scale depends on usage and plan details that are not in this dataset, so confirm with each vendor.

faq.q: Can I migrate from np to Capacitor?
faq.a: np is tagged with low lock-in (oss npm release cli). Capacitor is tagged with low lock-in (open-source cross-platform framework). Both sides are low lock-in, so a migration is feasible but still needs planning around data export and SDK rewrites.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, np or Capacitor?
faq.a: Both are rated 5/5 for developer experience in this dataset. With equal scores, the choice comes down to the specific SDK surface and docs you will use day to day. Run a short spike on each before deciding.

faq.q: Is Capacitor a good alternative to np?
faq.a: Capacitor is listed as fitting mobile, which overlaps with but is not identical to np's scope. Capacitor is flagged as not for internal projects, so confirm your context does not fall there.
